Description: This category covers all things related to your heart and the network of blood vessels (arteries and veins) throughout your body. Think of it as the body's plumbing and pumping system. It includes common issues like high blood pressure, heart attacks, clogged arteries, heart failure, strokes, and problems with heart rhythms. Keeping this system healthy is crucial for delivering oxygen and nutrients to every part of you.

This is the specialty of your skin, hair, and nails—your body's outer layer and first line of defense. It deals with everything from temporary rashes, acne, and infections to chronic conditions like eczema and psoriasis, as well as more serious issues like skin cancer. If it affects your complexion, causes itching, or changes a mole, it falls under dermatology.

Often called ENT, this category focuses on the interconnected systems of your head and neck that you use to hear, smell, taste, breathe, and swallow. It includes ear infections, hearing loss, sinus troubles, allergies, sore throats, tonsillitis, voice box problems, and even issues with balance. It's all about the health of your senses and airways in that region.

This deals with your body's hormone system. Hormones are chemical messengers produced by glands (like the thyroid, pancreas, and adrenal glands) that control your metabolism, energy levels, growth, mood, and reproduction. Common disorders include diabetes (problems with insulin), thyroid issues (which can make you feel sluggish or wired), and growth disorders.

This covers your entire digestive tract, from your mouth to your stomach, intestines, liver, gallbladder, and pancreas. It's about how your body processes food, absorbs nutrients, and gets rid of waste. Issues can range from common heartburn and stomach ulcers to irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), Crohn's disease, food intolerances, and liver disease.

These are health conditions caused by changes or mutations in your genes, the blueprint you inherited from your parents. Some are present from birth (like Down syndrome or cystic fibrosis), while others might increase your risk of developing certain diseases later in life (like some inherited cancers). They are not contagious but can be passed down in families.

This is all about blood. It includes disorders that affect your red blood cells (like anemia, which causes fatigue), white blood cells (which fight infection), platelets (which help with clotting), and the plasma part of blood. Conditions include various types of anemia, clotting disorders like hemophilia, and blood cancers like leukemia.

This category is specifically focused on the liver, one of your body's hardest-working organs. The liver filters toxins from your blood, helps digest food, stores energy, and makes proteins. Diseases here include hepatitis (liver inflammation), fatty liver disease, cirrhosis (scarring), and liver failure, often caused by viruses, alcohol, or obesity.

This involves your immune system, your body's defense army against germs. Problems occur when this army malfunctions. It includes allergies (overreactions to harmless things like pollen), autoimmune diseases (where the body mistakenly attacks itself, like in rheumatoid arthritis or lupus), and immunodeficiency disorders (where the immune system is too weak).

These are illnesses you can "catch" because they are caused by invading germs like bacteria, viruses, fungi, or parasites. They can be spread from person to person, through insect bites, or via contaminated food or water. Examples range from the common cold, flu, and COVID-19 to more serious diseases like malaria, tuberculosis, and HIV/AIDS.

Metabolism is the process your body uses to convert food into energy. Metabolic disorders disrupt this process, meaning your body might not produce enough energy or might produce too much of certain substances. The most well-known example is diabetes, but it also includes conditions like gout, metabolic syndrome, and disorders that affect how your body processes fats and proteins.

This covers the framework that holds you up and lets you move: your bones, muscles, joints, ligaments, and tendons. It includes everyday aches and pains like back strain, arthritis (joint inflammation), osteoporosis (weak bones), injuries like fractures and sprains, and diseases that affect muscle strength.

This concerns your body's command center: the brain, spinal cord, and all the nerves that branch out to every part of your body. It includes conditions that affect how you think, move, feel, and sense the world. Examples are headaches, epilepsy, strokes, Alzheimer's disease, Parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis (MS), and nerve damage.

This is the study and treatment of cancer. Cancer is a disease where abnormal cells grow uncontrollably and can invade other tissues. It can develop in almost any part of the body (e.g., breast, lung, colon, blood) and form tumors. Oncology deals with diagnosing these cancers, treating them (with chemo, radiation, surgery, etc.), and providing care.

This is the specialty of medical and surgical care for your eyes and vision. It includes common problems like nearsightedness and farsightedness, more serious conditions like glaucoma and cataracts, eye injuries, and diseases that can affect the eye, like diabetic retinopathy.

This focuses on health within your mouth, including your teeth, gums, tongue, and the roof and floor of your mouth. It covers tooth decay (cavities), gum disease (gingivitis and periodontitis), oral cancers, canker sores, and how oral health is connected to your overall health.

This isn't about a specific body part but about the unique medical needs of infants, children, and adolescents. Children are not just small adults; they grow and develop in specific ways. This category includes childhood vaccines, developmental disorders, common childhood illnesses, and conditions that are specifically seen in or are different in younger people.

This involves the organs and hormones responsible for having children. For women, this includes the uterus, ovaries, and breasts, covering conditions like endometriosis, fibroids, and ovarian cysts. For men, it involves the prostate and testes. It also includes sexual health, infertility, and menopause.

This covers the system that allows you to breathe—your lungs, airways (windpipe and bronchial tubes), and the muscles that help you inhale and exhale. Common conditions include asthma, bronchitis, pneumonia, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and lung cancer.

This is the study of poisons, chemicals, and overdoses. It deals with the harmful effects of substances, whether they are swallowed, inhaled, or absorbed through the skin. This includes accidental poisonings (like a child eating medication), drug overdoses, venomous bites, and exposure to environmental toxins or hazardous chemicals.

Also known as the renal system, this includes your kidneys, bladder, and the tubes that carry urine (ureters and urethra). It's your body's filtration and waste-removal system for liquids. Problems here include urinary tract infections (UTIs), kidney stones, bladder control issues (incontinence), and kidney disease or failure.

This category deals with your emotional, psychological, and social well-being. It affects how you think, feel, and act. Conditions include depression, anxiety disorders, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), and addiction. Mental health is just as critical as physical health for your overall quality of life.

This refers to conditions that are a direct result of or related to a surgical procedure or operation. This can include post-operative infections, complications from anesthesia, problems with healing at the incision site, blood clots after surgery, or pain management during recovery.

This system is a network of tissues and organs (including lymph nodes, the spleen, and thymus) that help rid your body of toxins and waste. It's a key part of your immune system, carrying white blood cells throughout the body. Diseases in this category include lymphedema (swelling due to fluid buildup) and cancers like lymphoma.
